commit.message: Implement movprfx fusion optimization in MacroAssembler for aarch64

8376285: AArch64: Support fusion optimization for SVE destructive instructions

There are a lot of SVE instructions that require the destination
register to be the same as the first source register in order to save
encoding space. For example `add  z18.b, p0/m, z18.b, z17.b`. To meet
this requirement, sometimes we have to insert a `mov` instruction
before this kind of instructions. For example:
```
mov z16.b, z18.b
add z16.b, p0/m, z16.b, z17.b
```

SVE provides a special vector copy instruction `movprfx` as a hint to
the hardware that it can be fused with the subsequent destructive
instruction to form a single constructive operation [1] (section 4.17).
Therefore, we can use `movprfx` instead of a normal `mov` instruction to
achieve better performance.

For the above example, it becomes:
```
movprfx z16, z18
add     z16.b, p0/m, z16.b, z17.b
```
The above two instructions will be fused as a single constructive
instruction at the hardware level, which behave identically to:
```
add z16.b, p0/m, z18.b, z17.b
```

This PR enables the fusion optimization for such kind of SVE
instructions. Specifically it replaces `sve_orr`/`mov` with `movprfx`
for SVE during code generation.

In some cases, a `mov` instructions is inserted by register allocator
for various match rules, the same change is also applied to the
implementation of `MachSpillCopyNode` to cover those cases. Note that
this does not change the logic of register spill; it only changes the
code generation of `MachSpillCopyNode` from "mov" to "movprfx".

However, since we can't accurately identify SVE destructive instructions,
this change may result in a non-SVE destructive instruction following
movprfx, so the fusion optimization will not take effect. But this has
no impact. When fusion fails, `movprfx` works as a normal register `mov`
instruction, and `movprfx` and `mov` have the same latency and throughput.

With this change we can see 1.11x–1.37× performance improvement in some
JMH cases, for example:

On a Nvidia Grace (Neoverse-V2) machine with 128-bit SVE2:
```
Benchmark			size	Unit	Before	Error	After	Error	Uplift
rearrangeFromByteVector		1024.00	ops/ms	11668.9 43.92	15949.5 6.16	1.37
rearrangeFromByteVector		2048.00	ops/ms	5874.90	16.14	8014.33	3.88	1.36
rearrangeFromDoubleVector	1024.00	ops/ms	1354.00	7.08	1738.17	2.05	1.28
rearrangeFromDoubleVector	2048.00	ops/ms	673.46	0.07	870.83	0.61	1.29
rearrangeFromFloatVector	1024.00	ops/ms	2502.55	14.56	3224.70	1.00	1.29
rearrangeFromFloatVector	2048.00	ops/ms	1242.57	0.99	1609.34	0.70	1.30
rearrangeFromIntVector		1024.00	ops/ms	2950.16	6.06	4001.44	10.97	1.36
rearrangeFromIntVector		2048.00	ops/ms	1544.66	7.90	1999.36	1.21	1.29
rearrangeFromLongVector		1024.00	ops/ms	1520.42	2.97	1999.07	1.43	1.31
rearrangeFromLongVector		2048.00	ops/ms	745.88	0.15	1000.13	0.56	1.34
rearrangeFromShortVector	1024.00	ops/ms	5914.31	26.82	7996.63	7.81	1.35
rearrangeFromShortVector	2048.00	ops/ms	2944.97	7.08	4002.63	9.96	1.36
```

On an AWS Graviton3 (Neoverse-V1) machine with 256-bit SVE1:
```
Benchmark		    size    Unit    Before  Error   After   Error   Uplift
rearrangeFromLongVector     2048.00 ops/ms  706.91  0.76    795.26  0.53    1.12
rearrangeFromShortVector    1024.00 ops/ms  5612.99 5.01    6322.04 9.21    1.13
rearrangeFromShortVector    2048.00 ops/ms  2821.90 1.42    3172.05 1.47    1.12
rearrangeFromByteVector     1024.00 ops/ms  11095.8 21.3    12298.5 26.55   1.11
rearrangeFromByteVector     2048.00 ops/ms  5580.39 5.33    6275.13 12.07   1.12
rearrangeFromDoubleVector   1024.00 ops/ms  1251.41 3.21    1475.29 2.98    1.18
rearrangeFromDoubleVector   2048.00 ops/ms  625.68  0.55    731.09  1.90    1.17
rearrangeFromFloatVector    1024.00 ops/ms  2464.68 2.89    2885.88 9.35    1.17
rearrangeFromFloatVector    2048.00 ops/ms  1229.27 1.74    1447.10 4.52    1.18
rearrangeFromIntVector      1024.00 ops/ms  2822.40 2.01    3167.00 2.80    1.12
rearrangeFromIntVector      2048.00 ops/ms  1418.65 0.78    1591.61 1.87    1.12
rearrangeFromLongVector     1024.00 ops/ms  1417.56 1.48    1592.04 1.58    1.12
```

To measure the broader impact of this PR, we tested the stock-vector [2]
JMH and the JMH benchmarks in the `openjdk/panama-vector` [3] project on
a Neoverse V2 and a Neoverse V1 machine using SVE2 and SVE1 respectively.
And to eliminate the impact of test fluctuations, we conducted tests
with different fork values (`-f 1`, `-f 3` and `-f 5`). The merged
results are as follow:

```
Config    Benchmark suite  Total number  Improvements (>= 5%)  Regression (>= 5%)
V2 SVE2   panama-vector    720           17                    3
V1 SVE1   panama-vector    720           25                    0
V2 SVE2   stock-vector     751           20                    6
V1 SVE1   stock-vector     881           78                    6
```
The overall performance improvement range is `0% ~ +47%`, and the
degradation range is much smaller, mostly around 5%, with one exception
of 15% for `IntMaxVector.ROL`. Analysis revealed that almost all
regressions were caused by test fluctuations. For `IntMaxVector.ROL`,
the regression only occurs on V2, with a specific input array size and
unrolling level. This may be caused by specific micro architecture
behavior and we think it's acceptable given that the change benefits
almost all other cases.

This PR was tested on different aarch64 machines (Neoverse-V2/V1) with
different features (SVE2, SVE1, NEON), and all tests (tier1, tier2 and
tier3) passed.

[1] https://documentation-service.arm.com/static/668bc0a369e89f01e39c4668?token=
[2] https://github.com/openjdk/jdk/tree/master/test/micro/org/openjdk/bench/jdk/incubator/vector
[3] https://github.com/openjdk/panama-vector/tree/vectorIntrinsics/test/micro/org/openjdk/bench/jdk/incubator/vector/operation
